ratings.rationale: Extremely familiar holiday imagery with four clean, everyday categories and almost no overlap or ambiguity.
Still concrete, but Maslenitsa weekday names and some traditions are more culturally specific and easier to mix up than the food sets.
Mostly familiar seasonal material, though Easter items and spring phenomena require a bit more thematic sorting than the easiest sets.
The categories are clear, but historical knowledge and culturally loaded symbols make it less automatic for a typical player.
Very everyday school vocabulary, though a couple of bag-related items create mild overlap between stationery and what sits in a backpack.
School-subject groupings are highly recognizable and the category borders are broad enough to feel straightforward.
Transport is concrete, but several items can plausibly fit multiple transport lenses, creating moderate false trails.
The transport domains are intuitive, yet some entries are less everyday and a few distinctions feel taxonomy-based rather than obvious.
Nature terms are familiar overall, but the boundary between weather, sky events, and disasters can blur.
Seasonal signs are a very familiar schema and most entries strongly telegraph their season.
This one leans on color theory and naming precision, with nonstandard rainbow choices creating notable false-trail pressure.
Fruit categories are common, though semantic precision like stone-fruit versus pome adds some mild classification work.
Mostly accessible grocery knowledge, but botanical versus culinary vegetable groupings raise the difficulty slightly.
Berry subtypes and poisonous examples are less universally known, so obscurity rather than abstraction drives the challenge.
Mushroom taxonomy and dangerous look-alikes demand specific cultural knowledge and invite strong confusion among similar-sounding items.
Animal categories are concrete and everyday, with only a little uncertainty around farm versus pet versus utility animals.
Wild-animal groupings are familiar and visually distinct, making the set easy to parse.
Bird categories are clear in principle, but ornithology knowledge and cross-category candidates add moderate difficulty.
Fish families and habitat labels are much less everyday knowledge, so a typical player may rely on weak guesses.
Insects are concrete, but some category labels are loose and several creatures can feel misclassified or overlapping.
Tree groupings are largely familiar, with only a small bump from regional and botanical precision.
Plant categories are intuitive and imageable, though a few items could tempt cross-placement between wild and cultivated settings.
Profession clusters are socially familiar and their functions are easy to distinguish.
Tool use is concrete, but specific instrument names and overlapping functions make the sorting less obvious than it first appears.
Instrument families are standard knowledge, though a couple of borderline cases require some music-class familiarity.
Furniture is everyday, but several pieces fit multiple use-based categories, creating mild ambiguity.
Kitchenware categories are practical and easy to visualize, with only limited overlap between serving and storage items.
Basic clothing vocabulary and body-location groupings make this one very easy inside the tier.
Shoe types are familiar, but fashion terminology and some cross-season usage raise the complexity a bit.
Breakfast foods split into very natural everyday groups with almost no hidden traps.
Russian soup names and subtype distinctions require food-specific knowledge, especially for less common traditional dishes.
Main-course groupings are sensible, but dish names and ingredient bases are not equally familiar to all players.
Bakery terminology is concrete yet culturally specific, and several flour-based items feel deceptively similar.
Dessert categories are recognizable, but Soviet/Russian candy names and pastry terms add recall difficulty.
Drink categories are mostly everyday, though regional beverage knowledge makes some entries less immediate.
Kitchen seasonings are common, but the boundaries between spice, herb, sauce, and seed are not always crisp.
Body-part groupings are highly concrete, with only a slight bump from anatomical vocabulary like phalanx.
Face anatomy is familiar overall, though nested subparts like parts of the eye require more precise attention.
Family relations are everyday, but in-law terms and extended-generation labels add some linguistic friction.
Rooms in a home are easy to picture and the functional categories are cleanly separated.
Appliance names are concrete, but modern kitchen devices can sound similar and sometimes serve overlapping purposes.
Household appliances are familiar, though a few modern gadgets and storage devices slightly complicate the grouping.
Store types are everyday knowledge and most items strongly signal their retail domain.
Finance terms are common enough, but abstract service categories and investment vocabulary make this tougher than ordinary household topics.
Sports categories are broad, well-known, and easy to separate for a typical player.
Winter sports require more specialized knowledge, and some ice-based categories compete with one another.
Sports equipment maps very directly onto familiar activities, so the set feels approachable.
Olympic-program knowledge is less everyday, and newer or umbrella categories demand more precise recall.
Childhood games are concrete but culturally specific, and several traditional game names are easy to confuse.
Toy categories are extremely familiar and visually distinct, making the grouping nearly automatic.
Board-game formats overlap heavily and some titles fit more than one play-style lens, creating strong false trails.
Basic computer and internet terms are mainstream and the categories are simple everyday digital knowledge.
Smartphone functions and apps are highly familiar to most players, with clean category cues.
Social-media vocabulary is current and accessible, though a few profile and engagement terms can blur together.
Film genres are recognizable, but labels mix formal genres with styles and modes, making the taxonomy less tidy.
Music genres depend on pop-culture familiarity, and several labels have fuzzy stylistic boundaries.
Theater vocabulary is fairly standard, though a few backstage terms are less common in everyday speech.
Circus imagery is vivid and the category splits are concrete and visually memorable.
Zoo animals are highly familiar and grouped into broad, intuitive clusters.
The top-level astronomy categories are clear, but moon names and dwarf planets require specific factual recall.
